text: Here we present a case of laparoscopic abdominal perineal resection following revascularization of the iliac vessels. The patient was a sixty-three year old man with low rectal cancer located just above the anal verge. The most important problem was the presence of arteriosclerosis obliterans. As shown here, right-sided arterial occlusion was observed from the common iliac artery to the femoral artery. In addition, the left common iliac artery was also severely stenotic. Before cancer treatment, revascularization of the left iliac artery was performed. A vascular stent was inserted into the left common iliac artery. Furthermore, femoro-femoral bypass surgery using an artificial blood vessel was performed from the left femoral artery to the right femoral artery.

Here we present a case of laparoscopic abdominal perineal resection following revascularization of the iliac vessels. The patient was a sixty-three year old man with low rectal cancer located just above the anal verge. The most important problem was the presence of arteriosclerosis obliterans. As shown here, right-sided arterial occlusion was observed from the common iliac artery to the femoral artery. In addition, the left common iliac artery was also severely stenotic. Before cancer treatment, revascularization of the left iliac artery was performed. A vascular stent was inserted into the left common iliac artery. Furthermore, femoro-femoral bypass surgery using an artificial blood vessel was performed from the left femoral artery to the right femoral artery.

The 3D imaging displays the tumor invading the levator ani muscle, left neurovascular bundle, and periosteum of the S-V sacrum. Despite preoperative chemoradiotherapy, the tumor remains invasive. The imaging shows the planned resection line of the sacrum, with complete occlusion of the right iliac arteries and maintained left iliac arteries.

The 3D imaging shows the tumor invading the levator ani muscle, left neurovascular bundle, and periosteum of the S-V sacrum. Despite preoperative chemoradiotherapy, the tumor remains invasive. The planned resection line of the sacrum is visible, with complete occlusion of the right-sided iliac arteries and maintained left iliac arteries.

The surgeon begins the incision on the right side of the upper mesorectum, dissecting the loose fibrous layer between the mesorectum and prehypogastric nerve fascia. Ventral traction is applied to the IMA pedicle, extending the fibrous tissues behind it. The peritoneum is incised up to the root of the IMA, which is clipped and divided. The left-sided colon is mobilized, and posterior dissection of the rectum continues until reaching the pelvic floor, with the right hypogastric nerve preserved.

The surgeon begins the incision on the right side of the upper mesorectum, dissecting the loose fibrous layer between the mesorectum and prehypogastric nerve fascia. Ventral traction is applied to the IMA pedicle, extending the fibrous tissues behind it. The peritoneum is incised up to the root of the IMA, which is then clipped and divided. The left-sided colon is mobilized, and posterior dissection of the rectum continues until reaching the pelvic floor, with the right hypogastric nerve preserved.

The surgeon divides the left hypogastric nerve and shifts the dissection layer outward. The left ureter is retracted from the external iliac artery and separated from the internal iliac artery. Dissection continues along the medial wall, revealing the superior vesicle artery, internal iliac vein, and inferior vesicle artery. Further dissection along this layer allows for the identification of the internal iliac vein and inferior vesicle vessels.

The surgeon divides the left hypogastric nerve and shifts the dissection layer outward. The left ureter is retracted from the external iliac artery and separated from the internal iliac artery. Dissection continues along the medial wall, revealing the superior vesicle artery, internal iliac vein, and inferior vesicle artery.

The surgeon identifies the tendinous arch of the levator ani muscle at the bottom of the field. The prehypogastric nerve fascia is divided, and the rectum is lifted. The left SIII and SIV pelvic splanchnic nerves are divided, while the right SIII and SIV nerves are preserved. The right side of the rectum is mobilized along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, and the seminal vesicle is visible on the anterior side of the rectum.

The surgeon identifies the tendinous arch of the levator ani muscle at the bottom of the surgical field. The prehypogastric nerve fascia is divided, and the rectum is lifted to divide the SIII and SIV pelvic splanchnic nerves on the left side, while preserving them on the right. The right side of the rectum is mobilized along the TME plane, with the seminal vesicle visible anteriorly. Dissection continues on the right side, preserving the S-IV nerve branch and neurovascular bundle, while the right side of the levator ani muscle is divided using electric cautery. The sacrum is partially divided using a soft coagulation method, and the left side of the levator ani muscle is divided to ensure adequate surgical margins.

The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, preserving the S-IV nerve branch and neurovascular bundle, while dividing the right levator ani muscle using electric cautery. The sacrum is partially divided using a soft coagulation method. On the left side, the levator ani muscle is divided to ensure adequate surgical margins, and dissection of the left rectum proceeds with preservation of the superior and inferior vesicle arteries. Fat tissues in the ischiorectal fossa are exposed after dividing the levator ani muscle. The proximal sigmoid colon is divided, and the patient's body position is changed to the jackknife position.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side of the rectum, preserving the superior and inferior vesicle arteries. The levator ani muscle is divided, exposing fat tissues in the ischiorectal fossa. The proximal sigmoid colon is divided, and the patient's position is changed to the jackknife position for a transperineal approach. Sacrectomy is performed at the S-five level, and the left side neurovascular bundle is resected under direct vision. The final view from the transabdominal side shows the pelvic autonomic nerves, the cut end of the sacrum, and the divided levator ani muscle.

The surgeon performs a sacrectomy at the level of the S-V sacrum using a transperineal approach, resecting the left side neurovascular bundle under direct vision. The final view from the transabdominal side is shown.
The surgeon visualizes the pelvic autonomic nerves, the cut end of the sacrum, and the levator ani muscle in the surgical field.

Final pathology showed T three N zero M zero stage two A with negative resection margins. Two weeks postoperatively, the patient was discharged without any adverse events. He underwent adjuvant chemotherapy comprising an S one plus CPT-Eleven regimen for three months. At thirty-six months after surgery, no recurrence or clinical symptoms were observed.
